infobox.value: [Left-wing](/wiki/Left-right_political_spectrum "Left-right political spectrum") | | --- | | * Far-left * [Left](/wiki/Left_politics "Left politics") * [Centre-left](/wiki/Centre-left_politics "Centre-left politics") | | [Centre](/wiki/Centrism "Centrism") | | * [Centre-left](/wiki/Centre-left_politics "Centre-left politics") * [Centrism](/wiki/Centrism "Centrism") * [Centre-right](/wiki/Centre-right_politics "Centre-right politics") | | [Right-wing](/wiki/Left-right_political_spectrum "Left-right political spectrum") | | * [Centre-right](/wiki/Centre-right_politics "Centre-right politics") * [Right](/wiki/Right_politics "Right politics") * [Far-right](/wiki/Far-right_politics "Far-right politics") | | Other | | * [Big tent](/wiki/Big_tent "Big tent") * [Radical centrism](/wiki/Radical_centrism "Radical centrism") * [Syncretism](/wiki/Political_syncretism "Political syncretism") |

are politics further to the left on the [political spectrum](/wiki/Left%E2%80%93right_political_spectrum "Left–right political spectrum") than the standard [political left](/wiki/Political_left "Political left"). In certain instances, *far-left* has been associated with various forms of [authoritarianism](/wiki/Authoritarianism "Authoritarianism"), [anarchism](/wiki/Anarchism "Anarchism"), [communism](/wiki/Communism "Communism"), and [Marxism](/wiki/Marxism "Marxism"), or are characterised as groups that advocate for [revolutionary socialism](/wiki/Revolutionary_socialism "Revolutionary socialism") and related [communist ideologies](/wiki/Communist_ideologies "Communist ideologies"), or [anti-capitalism](/wiki/Anti-capitalism "Anti-capitalism") and [anti-globalisation](/wiki/Anti-globalization "Anti-globalization"). [Far-left terrorism](/wiki/Far-left_terrorism "Far-left terrorism") consists of [extremist](/wiki/Extremist "Extremist"), [militant](/wiki/Militant "Militant"), or [insurgent](/wiki/Insurgent "Insurgent") groups that attempt to realise their ideals through [political violence](/wiki/Political_violence "Political violence") rather than using democratic processes.

Political alignment on the extreme end of left-wing politics

This article is about the alignment within left-wing politics generally. For the political current within Marxism specifically, see [Ultra-leftism](/wiki/Ultra-leftism "Ultra-leftism").

See also: [Hard left](/wiki/Hard_left "Hard left")

**Far-left politics** are politics further to the left on the [political spectrum](/wiki/Left%E2%80%93right_political_spectrum "Left–right political spectrum") than the standard [political left](/wiki/Political_left "Political left"). In certain instances, *far-left* has been associated with various forms of [authoritarianism](/wiki/Authoritarianism "Authoritarianism"), [anarchism](/wiki/Anarchism "Anarchism"), [communism](/wiki/Communism "Communism"), and [Marxism](/wiki/Marxism "Marxism"), or are characterised as groups that advocate for [revolutionary socialism](/wiki/Revolutionary_socialism "Revolutionary socialism") and related [communist ideologies](/wiki/Communist_ideologies "Communist ideologies"), or [anti-capitalism](/wiki/Anti-capitalism "Anti-capitalism") and [anti-globalisation](/wiki/Anti-globalization "Anti-globalization"). [Far-left terrorism](/wiki/Far-left_terrorism "Far-left terrorism") consists of [extremist](/wiki/Extremist "Extremist"), [militant](/wiki/Militant "Militant"), or [insurgent](/wiki/Insurgent "Insurgent") groups that attempt to realise their ideals through [political violence](/wiki/Political_violence "Political violence") rather than using democratic processes.

## Ideologies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Far-left_politics&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Ideologies")]

Far-left politics are the leftmost ideologies on the left of the [left–right political spectrum](/wiki/Left%E2%80%93right_political_spectrum "Left–right political spectrum"). They are a heterogeneous group of ideologies within [left-wing politics](/wiki/Left-wing_politics "Left-wing politics"), and wide variety exists between different far-left groups.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeithMarch2023173-1)[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201610–11-2) Ideologies such as [communism](/wiki/Communism "Communism") and [anarchism](/wiki/Anarchism "Anarchism") are typically described as far-left.[[3]](#cite_note-:62-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBackes2023a12-5)[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKrüusselmannWeggemans2023a58-6) Far-left politics is typically regarded as being to the left of [social democracy](/wiki/Social_democracy "Social democracy").[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201610–11-2)[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVisserLubbersKraaykampJaspers2014542-7) As with all political alignments, the exact boundaries of [centre-left](/wiki/Centre-left_politics "Centre-left politics") versus far-left within [left-wing politics](/wiki/Left-wing_politics "Left-wing politics") are not clearly defined and can vary depending on context.[[3]](#cite_note-:62-3)[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBackes2023a4-8) Far-left parties sometimes avoid anti-capitalist rhetoric to appeal to the centre-left, while the centre-left may invoke language of radicalism or incorporate some of its ideas.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch20119–10-9) The modern far-left distinguishes itself from [social democracy](/wiki/Social_democracy "Social democracy") through its inherent opposition to [capitalism](/wiki/Capitalism "Capitalism"), [neoliberalism](/wiki/Neoliberalism "Neoliberalism"), and [globalisation](/wiki/Globalization "Globalization").[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201110-10) Adherents to the far-left may be drawn to it by a desire to correct injustice and unfairness, often when it is directed toward oppressed demographics.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKrüusselmannWeggemans2023a60-11) Some who engage in far-left politics are interested in a [single issue](/wiki/Single-issue_politics "Single-issue politics") like environmentalism and do not subscribe to a broader far-left ideology.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKrüusselmannWeggemans2023a58-6)

Academic study of far-left politics often uses *radical left* as an all-encompassing term, though some far-left groups object to this usage as derogatory. *Extreme left* and *anti-capitalist* are also commonly used as synonyms for the far-left.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeithMarch2023174–175-12) The *radical left* and the *extreme left* are often used as equivalents, though some writers create distinctions between them.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBackes2023a7-13) *[Hard left](/wiki/Hard_left "Hard left")* may also be used.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch20119-14) Far-left political parties use a variety of descriptors for themselves, including workers', labour, socialist, communist, militant, and revolutionary parties.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeithMarch2023174-15) Far-left ideologies are typically derived from either anarchism or Marxism,[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJungkunz2023a141-16) and the two are frequently combined or integrated together.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBackes2023a12-5) These ideologies are considered radical due to their divergence from [capitalism](/wiki/Capitalism "Capitalism"), the [dominant ideology](/wiki/Dominant_ideology "Dominant ideology") of modern society.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKrüusselmannWeggemans2023a57,_63-17) They do not have a radical element in [communist states](/wiki/Communist_state "Communist state") despite being far-left in a global context.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKrüusselmannWeggemans2023a63-18)

A unified working class has traditionally been the focus of far-left movements. [Karl Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx") defined the working class in the 19th century to include all waged employees of all industries. The development of [middle management](/wiki/Middle_management "Middle management") and decline of the *[petite bourgeoisie](/wiki/Petite_bourgeoisie "Petite bourgeoisie")* complicated the definition over time.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201646-19) The modern European far-left overall has higher educational attainment than its far-right equivalent.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERooduijnBurgoonvan_Elsasvan_de_Werfhorst2017540-20) Students and intellectuals have often been inclined to support far-left politics. The far-left may appeal to independent producers or craftsmen who fear competition for large corporations. The unemployed, including the elderly and disabled, are associated with the working class as defined by the far-left because of the disadvantages they may face.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201646-19) The majority of left-leaning labourers preferred social democracy over far-left ideologies.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201648-21)

### Communism and Marxism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Far-left_politics&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Communism and Marxism")]

Main articles: [Communism](/wiki/Communism "Communism") and [Marxism](/wiki/Marxism "Marxism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Vladimir_Lenin_giving_a_speech.jpg/250px-Vladimir_Lenin_giving_a_speech.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vladimir_Lenin_giving_a_speech.jpg)

Vladimir Lenin giving a speech

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Mao_Proclaiming_New_China.JPG/250px-Mao_Proclaiming_New_China.JPG)](/wiki/File:Mao_Proclaiming_New_China.JPG)

Chairman [Mao Zedong](/wiki/Mao_Zedong "Mao Zedong") and leading revolutionaries [proclaim](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Proclamation of the People's Republic of China") the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/China "China") on 1 October 1949, is an iconic day of the [Chinese Communist Revolution](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Revolution "Chinese Communist Revolution").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Communist_Party_of_Britain_on_parade_at_Tolpuddle%2C_2015_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Communist_Party_of_Britain_on_parade_at_Tolpuddle%2C_2015_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Communist_Party_of_Britain_on_parade_at_Tolpuddle,_2015_(cropped).jpg)

Members of the far-left[[22]](#cite_note-22) [Communist Party of Britain](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Britain "Communist Party of Britain") at the [Tolpuddle Martyrs' Festival](/wiki/Tolpuddle_Martyrs%27_Festival "Tolpuddle Martyrs' Festival")

[Communism](/wiki/Communism "Communism") is the belief that humanity should abandon class divisions in favour of a [communist society](/wiki/Communist_society "Communist society") organised around the needs and abilities of its citizens. Modern communism is a form of [revolutionary socialism](/wiki/Revolutionary_socialism "Revolutionary socialism") based on support for the communist society described in the writing of [Karl Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx") and [Friedrich Engels](/wiki/Friedrich_Engels "Friedrich Engels"), known as [Marxism](/wiki/Marxism "Marxism").[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreslauer202111-23)[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvan_der_Linden202210-24) Since its introduction, elements of Marxism have become a common factor between most far-left groups.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGray2023a99-25) Communist ideology appears in a variety of forms, especially since the dissolution of the Soviet Union left communists without a unifying force.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201118-26)

Marxism specifically opposes capitalism, in contrast to anarchists who oppose both capitalism and the state in its entirety.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJungkunz2023a142-27) Marxism sees the [proletariat](/wiki/Proletariat "Proletariat") as the primary movers of the revolution. Marx and Engels observed that the working class of 19th century Europe had little influence on or attachment to their nations, and they espoused [socialist internationalism](/wiki/Socialist_internationalism "Socialist internationalism") that framed class struggle as an issue that united workers without regard to national affiliation. They considered socialist movements within a nation to be necessary only as a means to challenge that nation's bourgeoisie.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch2011149-28) Marx and Engels believed that the most developed nations were the most likely to see a communist revolution.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201113-29)

[Leninists](/wiki/Leninists "Leninists"), followers of Russian Marxist [Vladimir Lenin](/wiki/Vladimir_Lenin "Vladimir Lenin"), believe that capitalism should be replaced by a [dictatorship of the proletariat](/wiki/Dictatorship_of_the_proletariat "Dictatorship of the proletariat"), which would cause capitalism to degrade and quickly disappear.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201648-21) Leninist-led revolutions have imposed authoritarian rule over society despite invoking concepts of equality when seeking power. The [Bolshevik revolution](/wiki/Bolshevik_revolution "Bolshevik revolution") created [soviet councils](/wiki/Soviet_(council) "Soviet (council)") that were to serve as a democratic method of achieving the dictatorship of the proletariat.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201650-30) With the failure of revolutions in Western Europe, Leninism moved away from the belief that revolutions would occur in more developed nations through popular unrest. According to [Luke March](/w/index.php?title=Luke_March&action=edit&redlink=1 "Luke March (page does not exist)"), the ideology was unsuccessful in more developed nations, as the middle and upper classes were more established, and the unionist working class outnumbered any revolutionary peasantry.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201113-29)

[Marxism–Leninism](/wiki/Marxism%E2%80%93Leninism "Marxism–Leninism") has historically been a major far-left ideology, especially before the [dissolution of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Soviet_Union "Dissolution of the Soviet Union").[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeithMarch2023173-1) [Stalinism](/wiki/Stalinism "Stalinism") supports a [one-party state](/wiki/One-party_state "One-party state") with a [planned economy](/wiki/Planned_economy "Planned economy").[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201650-30) Stalin abandoned socialist internationalism and established his policy of [socialism in one country](/wiki/Socialism_in_one_country "Socialism in one country"), where the Communist Party of the Soviet Union prioritised its own stability. It abandoned efforts to spread communism to Western Europe, and it exercised control over the Communist parties of neighbouring countries to reinforce its own strength.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch2011149-28)

[Trotskyism](/wiki/Trotskyism "Trotskyism") emerged as a dissident current within the Communist movement after Stalin’s rise. It criticised Stalin’s belief in [socialism in one country](/wiki/Socialism_in_one_country "Socialism in one country") and the [totalitarian](/wiki/Totalitarian "Totalitarian") system of rule he established, arguing that the Soviet Union had become a [degenerated workers state](/wiki/Degenerated_workers_state "Degenerated workers state") and later that its satellites were [deformed workers states](/wiki/Deformed_workers_states "Deformed workers states"). Trotskyism’s main organisation was the [Fourth International](/wiki/Fourth_International "Fourth International"), although this has seen multiple splits and regroupings.

Conservative groups within Marxism–Leninism, such as the [Communist Party of Greece](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Greece "Communist Party of Greece") and the [Portuguese Communist Party](/wiki/Portuguese_Communist_Party "Portuguese Communist Party"), support revitalisation of Soviet-style government and adhere to a classical [Leninist](/wiki/Leninist "Leninist") interpretation of communism.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeithMarch2023175-31) Reform communists such as the Cypriot [Progressive Party of Working People](/wiki/Progressive_Party_of_Working_People "Progressive Party of Working People") retain Soviet-style organisation but adopt public involvement in government, the use of a market system, and acceptance of New Left policies.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeithMarch2023175-31) [Maoism](/wiki/Maoism "Maoism") developed as an alternative form of Marxist–Leninist-style vanguardism that emphasised anti-imperialism.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGray2023a100-32) Maoism sees the [peasantry](/wiki/Peasantry "Peasantry") as the primary movers of the revolution.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESánchez-Cuenca2023a112-33) This spawned [Guevarism](/wiki/Guevarism "Guevarism"), an ideology advocated by [Che Guevara](/wiki/Che_Guevara "Che Guevara") and [Régis Debray](/wiki/R%C3%A9gis_Debray "Régis Debray") which holds that [foco](/wiki/Foco "Foco") guerrilla warfare can serve the role of a vanguard instead of a formal vanguard party.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEÁlvarez2023b88-34)

Other Marxist ideologies developed in Western Europe. [Eurocommunism](/wiki/Eurocommunism "Eurocommunism") supports a reformist, democratic approach to achieving communism and opposes the ideology of the Soviet Union.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201649-35)[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201142-36) [Autonomism](/wiki/Autonomism "Autonomism") rejects organised movements and promotes direct personal action. While originating from communist thought, it became more anarchist as it developed.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESommier2023a195-37) [Left communism](/wiki/Left_communism "Left communism") or [ultra-leftism](/wiki/Ultra-leftism "Ultra-leftism") is a Marxist challenge to Marxism–Leninism.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESommier2023a194–195-38) The ultra-left believes communism should be allowed to rise naturally when the people of a given area are inspired to revolt, instead of through a planned revolution.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBackes2023a13–14-39)

[Latin American liberation theology](/wiki/Latin_American_liberation_theology "Latin American liberation theology") blends Catholicism and Marxism.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEÁlvarez2023b89-40)

### Anarchism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Far-left_politics&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Anarchism")]

Main article: [Anarchism](/wiki/Anarchism "Anarchism")

Anarchism is a far-left ideology that rejects all forms of authority, social hierarchy, or socially imposed control over the people. This manifests as a total rejection of the [state](/wiki/State_(polity) "State (polity)") in any form.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBackes2023a12-5)[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZúquete2023c257-41) Some describe it as a revolutionary ideology that seeks to overthrow capitalism at once instead of dismantling it over time.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201648-21) Some anarchists like [Mikhail Bakunin](/wiki/Mikhail_Bakunin "Mikhail Bakunin") and [Peter Kropotkin](/wiki/Peter_Kropotkin "Peter Kropotkin") advocate a society based on social relations and interdependence. [Individualist anarchism](/wiki/Individualist_anarchism "Individualist anarchism"), influenced by [Max Stirner](/wiki/Max_Stirner "Max Stirner") and [Pierre-Joseph Proudhon](/wiki/Pierre-Joseph_Proudhon "Pierre-Joseph Proudhon"), emphasizes the [individual](/wiki/Individual "Individual") and their [will](/wiki/Will_(philosophy) "Will (philosophy)") over external determinants such as groups, society, traditions, and ideological systems. [Green anarchism](/wiki/Green_anarchism "Green anarchism") is an ideology within anarchism, and some of its contemporary adherents argue that [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change") will [become apocalyptic](/wiki/Climate_apocalypse "Climate apocalypse").[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZúquete2023c271257,_271-42) [Anarcho-primitivism](/wiki/Anarcho-primitivism "Anarcho-primitivism") holds that civilisation is responsible for environmental damage and must be dismantled.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZúquete2023c257-41) [Insurrectionary anarchism](/wiki/Insurrectionary_anarchism "Insurrectionary anarchism") involves the use of individual cells or [affinity groups](/wiki/Affinity_groups "Affinity groups") to carry out guerrilla attacks against people and places associated with political or economic power.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZúquete2023c263-43) Opponents of civilisation may support [accelerationism](/wiki/Accelerationism "Accelerationism"), which endorses acts that will increase chaos and instability within society to bring about its downfall more quickly.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZúquete2023c271-44)

At the end of the nineteenth century, a minority of anarchists adopted tactics of revolutionary [political violence](/wiki/Political_violence "Political violence"), known as [propaganda of the deed](/wiki/Propaganda_of_the_deed "Propaganda of the deed").[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarshall1993633–636-45) The belief was that acts of violence would make the public more aware of grievances against the established political system, demonstrate that the state is vulnerable, provoke the state into responding to expose hypocrisy, and encourage others to carry out their own acts of violence.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESánchez-Cuenca2023a108–109-46) Even though many anarchists distanced themselves from these terrorist acts, infamy came upon the movement and attempts were made to prevent [anarchists immigrating to the US](/wiki/Anarchism_in_the_United_States "Anarchism in the United States"), including the *[Immigration Act of 1903](/wiki/Immigration_Act_of_1903 "Immigration Act of 1903")*, also called the Anarchist Exclusion Act.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarshall1993633–636LutzUlmschneider201946-47) By the turn of the 20th century, the terrorist movement had died down, giving way to [anarchist communism](/wiki/Anarchist_communism "Anarchist communism") and [syndicalism](/wiki/Syndicalism "Syndicalism"), while anarchism had spread all over the world.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoya2015327-48)[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELevy201116-49)

### New Left

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Far-left_politics&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: New Left")]

The far-left is sometimes divided into the [Old Left](/wiki/Old_Left "Old Left") and the [New Left](/wiki/New_Left "New Left").[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeithMarch2023175-31) The New Left was a broad political movement that emerged from the [counterculture of the 1960s](/wiki/Counterculture_of_the_1960s "Counterculture of the 1960s") and continued through the 1970s. It consisted of activists in the [Western world](/wiki/Western_world "Western world") who, in reaction to the era's liberal establishment, campaigned for freer lifestyles on a broad range of social issues such as [feminism](/wiki/Feminism "Feminism"), [gay rights](/wiki/LGBT_movements "LGBT movements"), [drug policy reforms](/wiki/Drug_liberalization "Drug liberalization"), and gender relations.[[50]](#cite_note-Carmines_and_Layman-50) The New Left differs from the traditional left in that it tended to acknowledge the struggle for various forms of [social justice](/wiki/Social_justice "Social justice"), whereas previous movements prioritized explicitly economic goals. However, many have used the term "New Left" to describe an evolution, continuation, and revitalization of traditional [leftist](/wiki/Left-wing_politics "Left-wing politics") goals.[[51]](#cite_note-Kaufman-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53)

Some who self-identified as "New Left"[[54]](#cite_note-New_Left-54) rejected involvement with the [labour movement](/wiki/Labour_movement "Labour movement") and [Marxism](/wiki/Marxism "Marxism")'s historical theory of [class struggle](/wiki/Class_conflict "Class conflict");[[55]](#cite_note-55) however, others gravitated to their own takes on established forms of Marxism, such as the [New Communist movement](/wiki/New_Communist_movement "New Communist movement") (which drew from [Maoism](/wiki/Maoism "Maoism")) in the United States or the [K-Gruppen](/wiki/K-Gruppen "K-Gruppen")[[a]](#cite_note-K-Gruppe-56) in the [German-speaking world](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_territories_where_German_is_an_official_language "List of countries and territories where German is an official language"). In the United States, the movement was associated with the [anti-war](/wiki/Anti-war_movement "Anti-war movement") college-campus protest movements, including the [Free Speech Movement](/wiki/Free_Speech_Movement "Free Speech Movement").

The movement fell into decline following the end of the Vietnam War, in part as the result of a covert U.S. government campaign to mobilize the CIA's [CHAOS](/wiki/Operation_CHAOS "Operation CHAOS") and FBI's [COINTELPRO](/wiki/COINTELPRO "COINTELPRO") to exacerbate existing fissions within the movement's most prominent groups, such as [Students for a Democratic Society](/wiki/Students_for_a_Democratic_Society "Students for a Democratic Society") and the [Black Panther Party](/wiki/Black_Panther_Party "Black Panther Party").[[56]](#cite_note-57)[[57]](#cite_note-58) European Green parties formed from the New Left in the 1960s and 1970s, but these are not traditionally considered far-left.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarter2023186,_188-59)[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201137–38-60)

## Positions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Far-left_politics&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Positions")]

### Economics and class

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Far-left_politics&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Economics and class")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Anti-capitalism_color%E2%80%94_Restored.png/250px-Anti-capitalism_color%E2%80%94_Restored.png)](/wiki/File:Anti-capitalism_color%E2%80%94_Restored.png)

"[Pyramid of Capitalist System](/wiki/Pyramid_of_Capitalist_System "Pyramid of Capitalist System")", a cartoon displaying a diagram of class hierarchy, published by the [Industrial Workers of the World](/wiki/Industrial_Workers_of_the_World "Industrial Workers of the World") in 1911

The goal of far-left politics is to create a [classless society](/wiki/Classless_society "Classless society").[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJungkunz2023a142-27)[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBackes2023a12-5) It seeks a [post-capitalist](/wiki/Post-capitalist "Post-capitalist") world without exploitation, oppression, or class inequality.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201648,_202-61) This is often described as [communist society](/wiki/Communist_society "Communist society"), though terms such as *socialism* or *democratisation* may be used to describe a similar concept.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201672,_202-62)

Under a communist society as envisioned by the far left, all [means of production](/wiki/Means_of_production "Means of production") would be [owned collectively](/wiki/Collective_ownership "Collective ownership") and resources would be subject to [distribution according to need](/wiki/To_each_according_to_his_need "To each according to his need"). The specific nature of this society is not strictly defined, but it is generally agreed among the far-left that it would be [self-governing](/wiki/Self-governing "Self-governing") and extend globally.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201648-21) The method to bring about communist society became the primary distinction among far-left ideologies as they developed, and ideas of how communist society should function changed over time.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201650-30) While the far-left historically opposed social democracy over its reformist nature, the post-Soviet far-left accuses social democracy of being too comfortable with [neoliberalism](/wiki/Neoliberalism "Neoliberalism").[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201610–11-2)

Far-left groups support [redistribution of income and wealth](/wiki/Redistribution_of_income_and_wealth "Redistribution of income and wealth")[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVisserLubbersKraaykampJaspers2014542-7) and advocate [equality of outcome](/wiki/Equality_of_outcome "Equality of outcome") over [equal opportunity](/wiki/Equal_opportunity "Equal opportunity").[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201110-10) Equal rights are typically given higher priority than individual rights.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJesse2023a174-63) It argues that capitalism and [consumerism](/wiki/Consumerism "Consumerism") cause [social inequality](/wiki/Social_inequality "Social inequality") and advocate their dissolution. Some far-left groups also support the abolition of [private property](/wiki/Private_property "Private property").[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVisserLubbersKraaykampJaspers2014542-7) Far-left movements became increasingly [anti-industrialist](/w/index.php?title=Anti-industrialist&action=edit&redlink=1 "Anti-industrialist (page does not exist)") beginning in the 1960s.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESommier2023a198-64) The far-left rejects neoliberalism, but it also rejects centre-left ideas like social democracy and [Keynesian economics](/wiki/Keynesian_economics "Keynesian economics").[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeithMarch2023175-31) It supports social advances within capitalism, but only as temporary measures until capitalism's abolition.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201648-21) It expresses support for groups that are otherwise excluded from economic prosperity.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVisserLubbersKraaykampJaspers2014542-7)

As an extension of left-wing politics, the far-left maintains that inequality is a fixable problem.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeithMarch2023174-15) European populist left politics share many of the values of centre-left politics, including [cosmopolitanism](/wiki/Cosmopolitanism "Cosmopolitanism"), [altruism](/wiki/Altruism "Altruism"), and [egalitarianism](/wiki/Egalitarianism "Egalitarianism").[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERooduijnBurgoonvan_Elsasvan_de_Werfhorst2017538-65) By the 21st century, the European far-left expressed interest in many political issues traditionally associated with [progressivism](/wiki/Progressivism "Progressivism"), including [cost of living](/wiki/Cost_of_living "Cost of living"), [housing shortages](/wiki/Housing_shortage "Housing shortage"), and [identity politics](/wiki/Identity_politics "Identity politics").[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDaviesZdjelar2023a38–39-66)

### Social and environmental issues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Far-left_politics&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Social and environmental issues")]

Communism has historically emphasised economics and class over social issues.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201135-67) In the 1970s and 1980s, far-left movements in Western Europe were increasingly defined by the [new social movements](/wiki/New_social_movements "New social movements"), which gave prominence to issues such as environmentalism, [animal rights](/wiki/Animal_rights "Animal rights"), [women's rights](/wiki/Women%27s_rights "Women's rights"), the [peace movement](/wiki/Peace_movement "Peace movement"). These ideas as a singular movement became less prominent in far-left politics as they were subsumed by green politics, but they are still disparately supported by many in the far-left.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch2011172–173-68) The American far-left advocates [social equity](/wiki/Social_equity "Social equity"), and it cites [white privilege](/wiki/White_privilege "White privilege") and [male privilege](/wiki/Male_privilege "Male privilege") as the causes of inequity.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZenn2023b59–60-69)

Far-left parties hold a variety of positions on environmentalism.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarter2023190-70) Far-left environmentalist movements may support their causes for the sake of all living things, or out of interest in social justice for those affected by environmental damage.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMareš2023b283-71) Those who support radical environmentalism might also support typical pro-environmental positions but demand that they be done rapidly instead of gradually.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMareš2023b279-72) Modern radical environmentalists maintain belief in the idea of [climate apocalypse](/wiki/Climate_apocalypse "Climate apocalypse"), which has been adopted by moderates in some nations as well.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMareš2023b283–284-73) The far-left opposed [nuclear power](/wiki/Nuclear_power "Nuclear power") in the 1980s on environmental grounds.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMareš2023b282-74) The anti-globalisation movement adopted environmentalism as one of its main causes as it developed in the 1990s and 2000s.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMareš2023b283-71)

### Government and revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Far-left_politics&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Government and revolution")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Ni_Una_Menos_from_Raqqa.png/250px-Ni_Una_Menos_from_Raqqa.png)](/wiki/File:Ni_Una_Menos_from_Raqqa.png)

[Internationals](/wiki/International_Freedom_Battalion "International Freedom Battalion") in the [IRPGF](/wiki/IRPGF "IRPGF") battalion with text in Spanish reading "Without women in the revolution there is no revolution".

The far-left is a form of [radical politics](/wiki/Radical_politics "Radical politics"), as it calls for fundamental change to the [capitalist](/wiki/Capitalist "Capitalist") socio-economic structure of society.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch20118–9-75) This distinguishes it from the [reformist politics](/wiki/Reformist_politics "Reformist politics") of the centre-left.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeithMarch2023174-15) Members of the far-left have varying opinions on revolution and the state.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201611-76) The far-left has different stances on democracy, but it opposes [liberal democracy](/wiki/Liberal_democracy "Liberal democracy").[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201110–11-77) Far-left groups sometimes support [direct democracy](/wiki/Direct_democracy "Direct democracy") or [participatory democracy](/wiki/Participatory_democracy "Participatory democracy") with emphasis on protecting the rights of those who are disenfranchised in liberal democracy.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201116-78) The revolutionary left supports total rebellion against capitalist governments.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeithMarch2023175-31) This form of far-left politics has become less common over time as revolutions failed to develop and far-left groups have become more willing to work within liberal capitalist nations. [Deradicalisation](/wiki/Deradicalisation "Deradicalisation") has become more common within the broader political left.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201651-79)[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeithMarch2023176-80)

[Uwe Backes](/wiki/Uwe_Backes "Uwe Backes") and [Eckhard Jesse](/wiki/Eckhard_Jesse "Eckhard Jesse") argue that, as a radical ideological system, the far-left opposes [political pluralism](/wiki/Pluralism_(political_philosophy) "Pluralism (political philosophy)").[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBackes2023a10-81)[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJesse2023a174-63) Academics such as [Hans Eysenck](/wiki/Hans_Eysenck "Hans Eysenck") and [Edward Shils](/wiki/Edward_Shils "Edward Shils") suggest that extremism is a better means to define ideology than the left–right spectrum, and that far-left ideas like [Bolshevism](/wiki/Bolshevism "Bolshevism") are related to far-right ideas like [Nazism](/wiki/Nazism "Nazism") because of their common opposition to political pluralism, [liberal democracy](/wiki/Liberal_democracy "Liberal democracy"), and [private enterprise](/wiki/Private_enterprise "Private enterprise").[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBackes2023a5-82) Opponents of this view argue that extreme ideologies are separated by social class or that the far-left supports non-democratic means only to a democratic end.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBackes2023a6–7-83) Some supporters of left-wing politics define support for democracy as equivalent to being left-wing, rejecting *extremist* label and arguing that [communist states](/wiki/Communist_state "Communist state") are actually far-right entities.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBackes2023a4–5-84) In developed nations where the proletariat has influence over society, communist groups opt for institutional compromise over revolution.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESánchez-Cuenca2023a112-33)

Some far-left groups, such as [Leninist](/wiki/Leninism "Leninism") parties, advocate the concept of [vanguardism](/wiki/Vanguardism "Vanguardism"), in which a select group forms a vanguard party to function as a revolution's leadership.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGray2023a91–92,_94-85) According to [Philip W. Gray](/w/index.php?title=Philip_W._Gray&action=edit&redlink=1 "Philip W. Gray (page does not exist)"), this holds that history is guided by advances in technology and ownership of the means of production, that oppression permeates all aspects of society, that there will be an [end of history](/wiki/End_of_history "End of history") where oppression no longer exists, and that there is a class enforcing this oppression that must be overcome because it cannot understand or accept the end of history.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGray2023a93–94-86) It emphasises that this system of oppression and revolution is something that could be studied scientifically, and the vanguard party asserts that it is able to understand this science.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGray2023a92–93-87) Gray says that vanguardism incorporates [democratic centralism](/wiki/Democratic_centralism "Democratic centralism"), where the party membership makes a decision together and then enforces it on all of its members.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201143-88) Gray says it presents the vanguard party's ideas as inevitable and allows contrary ideas to be dismissed as [false consciousness](/wiki/False_consciousness "False consciousness") that was imposed by oppressors.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGray2023a95–96-89)

### Geopolitics and global affairs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Far-left_politics&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Geopolitics and global affairs")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Fight_the_state%2C_not_wars.jpg/250px-Fight_the_state%2C_not_wars.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fight_the_state,_not_wars.jpg)

Anarchist protesters in [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston") opposing state-waged war

As part of the left-wing, far-left politics is associated with [internationalism](/wiki/Internationalism_(politics) "Internationalism (politics)") and it rejects loyalty to the working class of one nation at the expense of others.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeithMarch2023174-15)[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201650-30) During the Cold War, supporters of Stalinism often applied internationalism to mean loyalty to the Soviet Union,[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201650-30) while the post-Soviet far-left is especially associated with the [anti-globalisation movement](/wiki/Anti-globalisation_movement "Anti-globalisation movement").[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeithMarch2023179-90) The anti-globalist far-left supports regulation of international [free trade](/wiki/Free_trade "Free trade"), the restructuring or dissolution of international political organisations, increased environmental regulations, the abolition of debt owed by developing nations.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch2011173-91) Political philosophers like [Michael Hardt](/wiki/Michael_Hardt "Michael Hardt"), [Antonio Negri](/wiki/Antonio_Negri "Antonio Negri"), and [John Holloway](/wiki/John_Holloway_(sociologist) "John Holloway (sociologist)") have advocated a leaderless separation from international power structures through refusal to participate in modern systems.[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch2011177-92)

The far-left typically opposes major geopolitical institutions such as [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") and the [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund").[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201110-10) The European far-left is typically [Eurosceptic](/wiki/Eurosceptic "Eurosceptic") and opposes the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), either challenging its liberal orientation or rejecting the idea of a union entirely from the perspective of [left-wing nationalism](/wiki/Left-wing_nationalism "Left-wing nationalism").[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVasilopoulou2023302–303-93) Many support entry into the European Union but wish to reorganise or repurpose it.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeithMarch2023179-90)

The far-left is typically [anti-imperialist](/wiki/Anti-imperialist "Anti-imperialist"), although during the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") supporters of the Eastern and Western blocs were often more accepting of their own side's actions.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201650-30) Although [Third camp](/wiki/Third_camp "Third camp") socialists opposed both blocs,[[93]](#cite_note-a021-94)[[94]](#cite_note-o627-95) many on the far left specifically oppose imperialist actions and military activity by the [Western world](/wiki/Western_world "Western world") and especially from the United States.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJungkunz2023a142-27) The far-left may support [militancy](/wiki/Militancy "Militancy") while also opposing [militarist](/wiki/Militarist "Militarist") ideas.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201650-30) During the Cold War, far-left groups associated with new social movements advocated for the interests of the [Third World](/wiki/Third_World "Third World").[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch2011172–173-68) Far-left movements in Europe generally support [Kurdish nationalism](/wiki/Kurdish_nationalism "Kurdish nationalism") and provide assistance to militant Kurdish groups.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDaviesZdjelar2023a38-96)

## Electoral dynamics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Far-left_politics&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Electoral dynamics")]

Far-left parties have historically been unable to win control of the government in parliamentary systems unless they join coalitions with social democratic governments.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeithMarch2023178-97) Whether to work with other parties in an electoral system is a common issue faced by far-left parties.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoughOlsenKoss20103-98) They are commonly unsuccessful in enacting policy in times where they do lead the government.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeithMarch2023178–179-99) The far-left does not benefit from an [incumbency advantage](/wiki/Incumbency_advantage "Incumbency advantage") as much as other ideological groups and is more likely to fail in reelection efforts.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeithMarch2023179-90) As with far-right parties, far-left parties are typically forced to moderate in democratic systems to gain support among voters, leading to factionalism as they disagree on how much to moderate.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201113–14-100)[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoughOlsenKoss201011-101) Far-left parties may be challenged by right-wing parties with [welfare chauvinism](/wiki/Welfare_chauvinism "Welfare chauvinism"), which supports a welfare state but restricts it from those seen as foreigners.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201138-102)

The far-left gain more support in nations with long-term [social inequality](/wiki/Social_inequality "Social inequality"),[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeithMarch2023176-80) and when there are poor economic conditions.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeithMarch2023178-97) Far-left voters are more likely to be working class, trade union members, and irreligious.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeithMarch2023178-97) Older, working class, male, and less educated voters are more likely to support communism over democratic socialism.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeithMarch2023178-97) The far-left primarily competes with [social democratic](/wiki/Social_democratic "Social democratic") parties for votes in electoral systems.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeithMarch2023176-80)[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBremer2023167-103) Green parties sometimes provide electoral competition for the far-left, as both groups appeal to similar demographics.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarter2023190-70) They vary in how willing they are to work alongside centre-left parties in electoral politics, which is a major point of dispute within many far-left groups. Alignment with centre-left parties sometimes causes far-left parties to moderate their positions.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201671–73-104) Strengthening of the far-left in a democratic system is associated with the weakening of democracy as it rejects political pluralism.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBackes2023a11-105)

Far-left politics often has a sizeable non-electoral aspect, made up of trade unions and social movements.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti20166–7-106) The far-left has historically supported direct activism over electoral gains, seeing it as a better position to improve workers' rights and build support for communist society.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201652–53-107) Movements in democratic nations may disagree over whether to participate in electoral politics, with some adhering to the Leninist belief that [bourgeois](/wiki/Bourgeoisie "Bourgeoisie") governments should be overthrown.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201643-108) Ideologies such as anarchism, left-communism, and some New Left positions reject electoral participation entirely.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201652-109) When European far-left parties have gained power, they generally moved away from non-electoral activism and used their influence to limit its reach.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201653-110) Among the Western European far-left, support for electoral participation increased throughout the 20th century as revolution appeared unfeasible.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201645–46-111) Far-left parties in Europe are often affiliated with the [Party of the European Left](/wiki/Party_of_the_European_Left "Party of the European Left").[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeithMarch2023174-15) The far-left is historically minuscule in Southeast Asia where it has been repressed or failed to develop.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUfen2023377-112)

Communist parties were the most common far-left parties between the 1920s and the 1960s, and in many cases, they were the only ones. Many other far-left parties emerged in the 1960s, including socialist and left-wing nationalist parties.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201654-113) As industrial workers became less common by the 21st century, far-left parties seeking power have been forced to either define the working class more loosely or to form alliances with other classes and ideological groups.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201115-114)

State repression can have unpredictable effects on far-left groups: Japan eliminated the domestic anarchist movement in the early-20th century, while Greece and the Czech Republic inadvertently strengthened theirs through attempts to repress them.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKrüusselmannWeggemans2023a63-18)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Far-left_politics&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [History of socialism](/wiki/History_of_socialism "History of socialism"), [History of communism](/wiki/History_of_communism "History of communism"), and [History of anarchism](/wiki/History_of_anarchism "History of anarchism")

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Far-left_politics&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Early history")]

Societies resembling communist society have been postulated throughout human history, and many have been proposed as the earliest socialist or communist ideas.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown2013364-115)[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENewman20056-116) The ideas of [Plato](/wiki/Plato "Plato") have been described as an early type of socialism.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENewman20056-116) In medieval Europe, some philosophers argued that [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus") believed in shared ownership of property and that the hierarchy of the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") was contrary to his teachings. This included the [Taborites](/wiki/Taborites "Taborites"), who attempted to create a social structure that resembled a communist society.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown2013364-115) Numerous [emancipation](/wiki/Emancipation "Emancipation") movements have occurred throughout history, including [slave rebellions](/wiki/Slave_rebellion "Slave rebellion") and [peasant revolts](/wiki/List_of_peasant_revolts "List of peasant revolts").[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201630-117)

Early examples of communist societies in fiction include *[Utopia](/wiki/Utopia_(More_book) "Utopia (More book)")* by [Thomas More](/wiki/Thomas_More "Thomas More"), which proposed a society without personal property, and *[The City of the Sun](/wiki/The_City_of_the_Sun "The City of the Sun")* by [Tommaso Campanella](/wiki/Tommaso_Campanella "Tommaso Campanella"), which proposed a society without the [family unit](/wiki/Family_unit "Family unit").[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown2013365-118)

Far-left politics comes from the left wing of the left–right political spectrum, which developed during the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution").[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBackes2023a4-8) Classical/historical far-left politics encompasses a range of [classical radical](/wiki/Classical_radicalism "Classical radicalism") ideologies that advocate for political change. While the modern far-left tends to be socialist, the classical far-left is ultra-radical, such as [Hébertists](/wiki/H%C3%A9bertists "Hébertists") during the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution")[[118]](#cite_note-119) and [Estrema Radicale](/wiki/Historical_Far_Left "Historical Far Left") in the [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy"). Historical far-left ideologies included [republicanism](/wiki/Classical_republicanism "Classical republicanism"); while not necessarily limited to radical left-wing politics today, it was classified as "far-left" during the period when monarchy was the global norm. At that time, the centre-left typically supported [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy"), leaving those advocating for a republic at the extreme end of the political spectrum.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBobbio199633-120)

Modern far-left politics developed from support for socialism.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201648-21) This can be traced to Europe and North America in the late 18th century, when industrialisation and political upheaval caused discontent among the working class.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvan_der_Linden20227–8-121) Socialists were those who objected to the changing social and economic structures associated with industrialisation, in that they promoted [individualism](/wiki/Individualism "Individualism") over [collectivism](/wiki/Collectivism "Collectivism") and that they created wealth for some but not for others, creating [economic inequality](/wiki/Economic_inequality "Economic inequality").[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENewman20056-116)

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Far-left_politics&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: 19th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Bachunin_sitting.jpg/250px-Bachunin_sitting.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bachunin_sitting.jpg)

[Mikhail Bakunin](/wiki/Mikhail_Bakunin "Mikhail Bakunin") in 1863

Early European socialism was developed in the 19th century as the concept of a [working class](/wiki/Working_class "Working class") formed. It was influenced by numerous philosophers, such as [Mikhail Bakunin](/wiki/Mikhail_Bakunin "Mikhail Bakunin"), [Louis Blanc](/wiki/Louis_Blanc "Louis Blanc"), [Louis Auguste Blanqui](/wiki/Louis_Auguste_Blanqui "Louis Auguste Blanqui"), [Henri de Saint-Simon](/wiki/Henri_de_Saint-Simon "Henri de Saint-Simon"), [Friedrich Engels](/wiki/Friedrich_Engels "Friedrich Engels"), [Charles Fourier](/wiki/Charles_Fourier "Charles Fourier"), [Ferdinand Lassalle](/wiki/Ferdinand_Lassalle "Ferdinand Lassalle"), [Karl Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx"), [Robert Owen](/wiki/Robert_Owen "Robert Owen"), and [Pierre-Joseph Proudhon](/wiki/Pierre-Joseph_Proudhon "Pierre-Joseph Proudhon"). The modern far left developed during the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") as their ideas were adopted as a response to [capitalism](/wiki/Capitalism "Capitalism") and [industrialisation](/wiki/Industrialisation "Industrialisation"). Marxism and anarchism joined reformist socialism as the predominant left-wing ideologies.[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201630-117) There were relatively few waged workers in the 19th century, which was still dominated by [subsistence agriculture](/wiki/Subsistence_agriculture "Subsistence agriculture") and independent sale of basic goods and services.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201646-19) The early far left was primarily made up of industrial workers.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201647-122) Labour groups led the [Revolutions of 1848](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1848 "Revolutions of 1848").[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201630-117) The [Luddites](/wiki/Luddite "Luddite") were anti-technology activists who emerged during the British [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), where they sabotaged machinery out of fear that it would displace workers.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZúquete2023c258-123)

The term *socialism* first came into use in the early 19th century to describe the egalitarian ideas of redistribution promoted by writers like [François-Noël Babeuf](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois-No%C3%ABl_Babeuf "François-Noël Babeuf") and [John Thelwall](/wiki/John_Thelwall "John Thelwall"). Inspired by the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"), these writers objected to the existence of significant wealth, and Babeuf advocated a dictatorship on behalf of the people that would destroy those who caused inequality.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvan_der_Linden20224–5-124)[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown2013365-118) Socialism was recognised as a coherent philosophy in the 1830s with the publications of British reformer Robert Owen, who self-identified as socialist.[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvan_der_Linden20225–6-125) Owen, as well as others such as Henri de Saint-Simon, Charles Fourier, and [Étienne Cabet](/wiki/%C3%89tienne_Cabet "Étienne Cabet"), developed the utopian socialist movement, and these utopian socialists established several communes to implement their ideology.[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvan_der_Linden20228-126)[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENewman20057–11-127) Cabet responded to More's *Utopia* with his own novel, *[The Voyage to Icaria](/wiki/The_Voyage_to_Icaria "The Voyage to Icaria")*.[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENewman20058-128) He is credited with first using the term *communism*, though his usage was unrelated to the ideologies that were later known as communism.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown2013366-129)

Early anarchists emerged in the 19th century, including Pierre-Joseph Proudhon and Mikhail Bakunin. These anarchists endorsed many utopian ideas, but they emphasised the importance of revolution against and complete abolition of the state for a utopian society to exist.[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENewman200515-130) Mikhail Bakunin developed early anarchism by creating the [Anti-authoritarian International](/wiki/Anti-authoritarian_International "Anti-authoritarian International") in 1872 after he challenged Marxism during the [Hague Congress](/wiki/Hague_Congress_(1872) "Hague Congress (1872)").[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESommier2023a194-131) He argued that peasants rather than the working class should lead a socialist revolution, and he popularised calls to violence among the anarchist movement.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENewman200517-132) Anarchist ideology spread to the Americas shortly after its development.[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvan_der_Linden202219–20-133)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Karl_Marx_001_%28rotated%29.jpg/250px-Karl_Marx_001_%28rotated%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Karl_Marx_001_(rotated).jpg)

[Karl Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx"), after whom Marxism is named.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Friedrich_Engels_portrait_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Friedrich_Engels_portrait_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Friedrich_Engels_portrait_(cropped).jpg)

[Friedrich Engels](/wiki/Friedrich_Engels "Friedrich Engels"), who co-developed Marxism.

Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels introduced [Marxism](/wiki/Marxism "Marxism") in the 1840s, which advocated [revolutionary socialism](/wiki/Revolutionary_socialism "Revolutionary socialism").[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvan_der_Linden20229–10-134)[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown2013366-129) As the state bureaucracy was developed in the late 19th century and [labour rights](/wiki/Labor_rights "Labor rights") were increasingly recognised by national governments, socialist movements were divided on the role of the state. Some objected to the increase in the state's involvement, while others believed that the state was a stronger alternative to protect worker's rights than labour movements.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvan_der_Linden202214–17-135) Many of the former moved to anarchism, while many of the latter responded with the development of social democracy.[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvan_der_Linden202218-136)

The Russian far-left group [Land and Liberty](/wiki/Land_and_Liberty_(Russia) "Land and Liberty (Russia)") arose in the 1860s to lead peasants' revolts against the monarchy. It split in 1879 between the populist movement [Black Repartition](/w/index.php?title=Black_Repartition&action=edit&redlink=1 "Black Repartition (page does not exist)") and the anarchist movement [Narodnaya Volya](/wiki/Narodnaya_Volya "Narodnaya Volya"). Narodnaya Volya engaged in acts of violence, called [propaganda of the deed](/wiki/Propaganda_of_the_deed "Propaganda of the deed"), to incite revolution. These groups were eradicated following the [assassination of Tsar Alexander II](/wiki/Assassination_of_Alexander_II_of_Russia "Assassination of Alexander II of Russia") in 1881, but the methods used by Narodnaya Volya were adopted by other groups and set the precedent for modern [terrorism](/wiki/Terrorism "Terrorism").[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDaviesZdjelar2023a24-137)

The [First International](/wiki/First_International "First International") was created in 1864 and lasted until 1872. The [Paris Commune](/wiki/Paris_Commune "Paris Commune") was created in 1871.[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201630-117) Many national trade unions were established in the 1880s, which coalesced into the [Second International](/wiki/Second_International "Second International") in 1889. This group was officially aligned with social democracy but was predominantly influenced by Marxism.[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201630-117)

[Mikhail Bakunin](/wiki/Mikhail_Bakunin "Mikhail Bakunin") was influential in developing anarchist ideas in the 19th century, advocating acts of violence instead of political discourse.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESánchez-Cuenca2023a107-138) [Errico Malatesta](/wiki/Errico_Malatesta "Errico Malatesta") introduced the [propaganda of the deed](/wiki/Propaganda_of_the_deed "Propaganda of the deed") in 1876 when he encouraged the use of violence in Italy to win the support of the working class.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESánchez-Cuenca2023a107-138)

### Early 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Far-left_politics&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Early 20th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Kropotkin_PA.jpg/250px-Kropotkin_PA.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kropotkin_PA.jpg)

[Peter Kropotkin](/wiki/Peter_Kropotkin "Peter Kropotkin") in 1917

East Asian anarchism developed in the 1900s during the [Russo-Japanese War](/wiki/Russo-Japanese_War "Russo-Japanese War"), based on the ideas of Japanese writer [Kōtoku Shūsui](/wiki/K%C5%8Dtoku_Sh%C5%ABsui "Kōtoku Shūsui"), who was in turn inspired by [Peter Kropotkin](/wiki/Peter_Kropotkin "Peter Kropotkin"). This movement saw its greatest prominence in the 1920s in China.[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvan_der_Linden202219–20-133) Anarcho-syndicalism was developed as a form of anarchism in the late 19th century, and it grew popular around 1900. It remained relevant in far-left politics through 1940.[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvan_der_Linden202220-139) One important syndicalist movement of this period was the [Wobblies](/wiki/Industrial_Workers_of_the_World "Industrial Workers of the World").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Left communism](/wiki/Left_communism "Left communism") was developed as a criticism of Marxism–Leninism by figures like [Rosa Luxemburg](/wiki/Rosa_Luxemburg "Rosa Luxemburg") and [Amadeo Bordiga](/wiki/Amadeo_Bordiga "Amadeo Bordiga") in the early-20th century.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESommier2023a194–195-38)

The modern revolutionary left emerged in the [aftermath of World War I](/wiki/Aftermath_of_World_War_I "Aftermath of World War I"). Socialist movements had gained considerable political power in Europe by the 1910s, but they were fractured during the war.[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201630-117) Before World War I, socialism was intertwined with the labour movement.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201650-30)

Moderate left-wing nationalist factions split from socialists in defence of their nations during the war, while the remaining far-left adopted a revolutionary [cosmopolitan](/wiki/Cosmopolitanism "Cosmopolitanism") ideology.[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201630-117) Opposition to World War I triggered [a series of revolutions](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1917%E2%80%931923 "Revolutions of 1917–1923") across Europe. Those in Finland, Germany, Hungary, and Russia were led by socialist movements. Trade unions, workers' councils, and far-left parties were formed in many European nations. Numerous far-left movements developed with different ideological foundations.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201630–31-140) The strongest far-left movement developed with the [Russian Revolution](/wiki/Russian_Revolution "Russian Revolution") and its establishment of Leninism.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201611-76) The [Bolsheviks](/wiki/Bolsheviks "Bolsheviks") seized power under the rule of Vladimir Lenin, and Lenin implemented the idea of vanguardism where the Bolsheviks were seen as continuing the revolution and preventing other economic systems from forming.[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGray2023a96–98-141) Italian anarchists created the first [car bomb](/wiki/Car_bomb "Car bomb") in 1920 to carry out the [Wall Street bombing](/wiki/Wall_Street_bombing "Wall Street bombing") in the United States.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESánchez-Cuenca2023a108-142) [Anton Pannekoek](/wiki/Anton_Pannekoek "Anton Pannekoek") developed what became [council communism](/wiki/Council_communism "Council communism") in 1920.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESommier2023a195-37) The Middle East developed an anti-colonial Marxist movement in the 1920s, where it spread from the [Russian Revolution](/wiki/Russian_Revolution "Russian Revolution").[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKraetzschmarResta2023415-143) Far-left politics emerged in Central America through the labour movement in the early years of the 20th century. Following the Russian Revolution, this shifted to focus on Marxism–Leninism.[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEÁlvarez2023b85-144) Central America had yet to industrialise by this point as Europe had, and it was still dominated by feudal-style land ownership, so most communists felt it had not yet reached the point of revolution.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEÁlvarez2023b86-145) [Italian futurism](/wiki/Futurism#Italian_Futurism "Futurism"), which was initially a radical left-wing ideology, had a profound effect on the growth of [Italian fascism](/wiki/Italian_fascism "Italian fascism").[[145]](#cite_note-146)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Protecci%C3%B3n_del_Palacio_Tauride_durante_el_Segundo_Congreso_Regional_de_los_Soviets.jpg/250px-Protecci%C3%B3n_del_Palacio_Tauride_durante_el_Segundo_Congreso_Regional_de_los_Soviets.jpg)](/wiki/File:Protecci%C3%B3n_del_Palacio_Tauride_durante_el_Segundo_Congreso_Regional_de_los_Soviets.jpg)

The dissolution of the [Constituent Assembly](/wiki/Russian_Constituent_Assembly "Russian Constituent Assembly") on 6 January 1918. The [Tauride Palace](/wiki/Tauride_Palace "Tauride Palace") is locked and guarded by [Trotsky](/wiki/Leon_Trotsky "Leon Trotsky"), [Sverdlov](/wiki/Yakov_Sverdlov "Yakov Sverdlov"), [Zinoviev](/wiki/Grigory_Zinoviev "Grigory Zinoviev") and [Lashevich](/wiki/Mikhail_Lashevich "Mikhail Lashevich").

Communism in early 20th century Europe often gained power in countries with significant polarisation between segments of the population on an ethnic, religious, or economic basis,[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201128-147) and in countries that were destabilised by war.[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreslauer20213-148) It was less prominent in industrialised nations, where social democracy maintained electoral success over Communist parties.[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201133-149) The Russian Revolution was the only instance of a successful socialist revolution during this period. Communist groups sought to emulate the Russian Revolution that replaced capitalism with a [planned economy](/wiki/Planned_economy "Planned economy") and established a system of [soviet councils](/wiki/Soviet_(council) "Soviet (council)") to serve as the dictatorship of the proletariat.[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201648–49-150) Other Communist governments were formed in [Bavaria](/wiki/People%27s_State_of_Bavaria "People's State of Bavaria"), [Finland](/wiki/Finnish_Socialist_Workers%27_Republic "Finnish Socialist Workers' Republic"), and [Hungary](/wiki/Hungarian_Soviet_Republic "Hungarian Soviet Republic"), but they were short-lived.[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201642-151) The Bolsheviks eventually became the [Communist Party of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_the_Soviet_Union "Communist Party of the Soviet Union").[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGray2023a96-152) Communist parties developed in other countries after the Russian Revolution, splitting from the dominant social democratic parties.[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoughOlsenKoss20105-153) Throughout its existence, the Soviet Union provided both material and non-material support to Communist parties in other nations, and it provided refuge for the leadership of parties in exile.[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201139–40-154) The Bolsheviks created the [Communist International](/wiki/Communist_International "Communist International") in 1919 to bring together the Communist parties of several nations, and the [International Working Union of Socialist Parties](/wiki/International_Working_Union_of_Socialist_Parties "International Working Union of Socialist Parties") existed from 1921 to 1923 for other socialist groups.[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201631-155) They hoped to join forces with Western social democrats, but the alliances were never formed.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201652-109) Support for immediate revolution declined among the far-left; it seemed less feasible as state intervention within capitalist nations brought about improvements in quality of life for the working class.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201649–50-156) The social democratic movement moderated, and much of the European far-left lost influence outside of Russia by 1923.[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201631-155)

By 1922, as [Russian SFSR](/wiki/Russian_SFSR "Russian SFSR") became one of the founding countries of [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), it responded to widespread hunger and poverty with the [New Economic Policy](/wiki/New_Economic_Policy "New Economic Policy"), which restored market enterprise for smaller industries.[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreslauer202156-157) After Lenin's death, a power struggle between [Nikolai Bukharin](/wiki/Nikolai_Bukharin "Nikolai Bukharin"), [Leon Trotsky](/wiki/Leon_Trotsky "Leon Trotsky"), and [Joseph Stalin](/wiki/Joseph_Stalin "Joseph Stalin") ended with Stalin taking power by 1928.[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreslauer202159–61-158) Stalin implemented his ideology of Marxism–Leninism, which reorganised society and created a cult of personality in his favour.[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreslauer202163–67-159) Under the rule of [Joseph Stalin](/wiki/Joseph_Stalin "Joseph Stalin"), the Soviet Union adopted [Stalinism](/wiki/Stalinism "Stalinism") in the 1930s.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201649-35) While the Communist International was initially democratic between its members, it removed disloyal parties while Stalin was in charge.[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201650–51-160) This also entailed the [Great Purge](/wiki/Great_Purge "Great Purge") in the late 1930s, an interpretation of Lenin's revolutionary violence that saw hundreds of thousands of Stalin's opponents killed, often to be replaced by ambitious loyalists.[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreslauer202171–72-161) By this time, Marxism–Leninism was seen as the definitive implementation of communism by its followers globally, justifying the Great Purge as an effort to eradicate fascist infiltrators, with state censorship obscuring the Great Purge's extent.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreslauer202191–92-162) This view was challenged by the [Anti-Stalinist left](/wiki/Anti-Stalinist_left "Anti-Stalinist left"), including anarchists and Trotskyists.[[162]](#cite_note-n426-163)[[163]](#cite_note-n612-164)[[164]](#cite_note-o562-165)

Western Europe largely adopted [liberal democracy](/wiki/Liberal_democracy "Liberal democracy") by the mid-1920s, and social democracy drew socialists to a more moderate stance. The far-left did not have significant political power and instead acted through labour movements, which engaged in strikes and insurrections.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201631–32-166) Its interest in communist revolution declined.[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201645-167) There was not always a clear delineation between the far-left and the centre-left this time as they were often affiliated with the same organisations.[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201637-168) Far-left parties in France, Germany, and Spain briefly took power in the 1930s but were eradicated as fascism spread across the continent.[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201632-169) The [Communist Party of Germany](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Germany "Communist Party of Germany") had split from the [Social Democratic Party of Germany](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Germany "Social Democratic Party of Germany") and became the largest Communist party in Western Europe, performing only 3.5 percent below the Social Democratic Party in the [November 1932 election](/wiki/November_1932_German_federal_election "November 1932 German federal election").[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201133-149)

The [Communist Party of El Salvador](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_El_Salvador "Communist Party of El Salvador") and the [Communist Party of Honduras](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Honduras "Communist Party of Honduras") both led unsuccessful indigenous and peasants' uprisings in their respective countries during the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"). These parties, along with the [Communist Party of Guatemala](/w/index.php?title=Communist_Party_of_Guatemala_(1922%E2%80%931932)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Communist Party of Guatemala (1922–1932) (page does not exist)"), were persecuted and largely dismantled as a result.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEÁlvarez2023b86-145)

How to respond to [fascism](/wiki/Fascism "Fascism") was a question that divided the far left in the interwar years. During its [ultra-left](/wiki/Ultra-leftism "Ultra-leftism") “[Third Period](/wiki/Third_Period "Third Period")”, the Communist International saw social democrats (who it labelled “[social fascists](/wiki/Social_fascism "Social fascism")”) as an equivalent enemy to Nazism. Trotsky, in contrast, argued for anti-fascist unity just within the far left, in the strategy of the [United front](/wiki/United_front "United front").[[169]](#cite_note-o785-170)[[170]](#cite_note-t814-171) Spain's far left launched the strongest response to the right when it fought the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War") from 1936 to 1939.[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201632-169) Far-left [popular front](/wiki/Popular_front "Popular front") groups arose in the mid-1930s.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201649-35)

During the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War") in the late 1930s, anarcho-syndicalists seized control of multiple regions in Spain, but this ended when the nationalist faction won the war.[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvan_der_Linden202223-172) This, along with the rise of Communism, ended the relevance of anarchism among the far-left globally after 1940.[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvan_der_Linden202220,_23-173) As [mass production](/wiki/Mass_production "Mass production") became more common, the traditional style of labour that anarcho-syndicalists objected to ceased to exist, preventing any significant resurgence in the movement.[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvan_der_Linden202223–24-174) Trotsky died in 1940, and Trotskyism subsequently underwent a period of lesser influence over the following two decades.[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201136-175)

Western opinion of Communists briefly improved in the [aftermath of World War II](/wiki/Aftermath_of_World_War_II "Aftermath of World War II") because of Communist contributions to the war effort, and they saw minor electoral success in a few European countries.[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201140-176) The [French Communist Party](/wiki/French_Communist_Party "French Communist Party") and [Italian Communist Party](/wiki/Italian_Communist_Party "Italian Communist Party") briefly became major parties in their respective nations, while the [Popular Democratic Front](/wiki/Popular_Democratic_Front_(Italy) "Popular Democratic Front (Italy)") of Italy and the [Finnish People's Democratic League](/wiki/Finnish_People%27s_Democratic_League "Finnish People's Democratic League") were formed as alliances between different far-left groups.[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201632–33-177) Italian Communists moved away from Leninism and democratic centralism in 1944 in favour of mass membership and Catholic influences.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201143-88) [Antonio Gramsci](/wiki/Antonio_Gramsci "Antonio Gramsci"), who was active in the 1920s and 1930s, became one of the main figures of Italian Communism.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201142-36) The [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") began shortly after, and Communist parties again became poorly regarded.[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201134-178) The Soviet-backed [Communist Party of Germany](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Germany "Communist Party of Germany") emerged from World War II as a minor party in [West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany"), but it lost popularity over the following years and was banned in 1956.[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJesse2023a176-179)

### Cold War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Far-left_politics&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Cold War")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Stalin_birthday2.jpg/250px-Stalin_birthday2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stalin_birthday2.jpg)

Members of the [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party") celebrating [Stalin](/wiki/Joseph_Stalin "Joseph Stalin")'s birthday in 1949

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Aldo_Moro_br.jpg/250px-Aldo_Moro_br.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aldo_Moro_br.jpg)

[Kidnapping and murder of Aldo Moro](/wiki/Kidnapping_and_murder_of_Aldo_Moro "Kidnapping and murder of Aldo Moro") by the [Red Brigades](/wiki/Red_Brigades "Red Brigades") (1978)

The [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") began when a major diplomatic rift occurred between liberal Western nations, led by the United States, and communist nations, led by the Soviet Union. Communist parties were effectively outcast within the West, and most were aligned with Stalinism and the Soviet Union.[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201633-180) The United States pressured other nations to purge Communist parties from their governments [in 1947](/wiki/May_1947_crises "May 1947 crises"), and the Italian government created its [conventio ad excludendum](/w/index.php?title=Conventio_ad_excludendum&action=edit&redlink=1 "Conventio ad excludendum (page does not exist)") [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/conventio_ad_excludendum "it:conventio ad excludendum")] that effectively disallowed the [Italian Communist Party](/wiki/Italian_Communist_Party "Italian Communist Party") from taking power.[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201134-178) The number of West European communists declined significantly, with only France, Italy, and briefly Finland retaining a notable communist presence. Under Soviet pressure, Eastern European communists remained dominant and became Stalinised.[[180]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201141-181) By the mid-1950s, the [Italian Socialist Party](/wiki/Italian_Socialist_Party "Italian Socialist Party") was the most influential anti-communist far-left party in Europe.[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201634-182) Violent revolution was discouraged as the Cold War began, emanating from fears that Western nations would intervene.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201652-109)

The Soviet Union's influence during and after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") spread Communism, directly and indirectly, to the rest of [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe") and into [Southeast Europe](/wiki/Southeast_Europe "Southeast Europe").[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreslauer202195,_104-183) Several of these countries became [people's democracies](/wiki/People%27s_democracy_(Marxism%E2%80%93Leninism) "People's democracy (Marxism–Leninism)"), which maintained some liberal [mixed economies](/wiki/Mixed_economies "Mixed economies") before eventually coming under the influence of Stalinism.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201649-35) New communist governments were formed in [Albania](/wiki/People%27s_Socialist_Republic_of_Albania "People's Socialist Republic of Albania"), [Bulgaria](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_Bulgaria "People's Republic of Bulgaria"), [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovak_Socialist_Republic "Czechoslovak Socialist Republic"), [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany"), [Hungary](/wiki/Hungarian_People%27s_Republic "Hungarian People's Republic"), [Romania](/wiki/Socialist_Republic_of_Romania "Socialist Republic of Romania"), and [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Socialist_Federal_Republic_of_Yugoslavia "Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia").[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreslauer202195,_104-183) Most far-left governments adopted the Leninist system of vanguardism. China and North Korea took on the Marxist—Leninist variant that developed under Stalin's rule of the Soviet Union.[[183]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGray2023a98-184) The development of [post-industrial society](/wiki/Post-industrial_society "Post-industrial society") and [postmaterialism](/wiki/Postmaterialism "Postmaterialism") in Western Europe caused many of the traditional sectors associated with communism to dissipate.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201135-67) Communist International had been dissolved in 1943, and it was replaced by [Cominform](/wiki/Cominform "Cominform") as the main communist international in 1947. This lasted until 1956.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201654-113)

While Soviet-style Communist parties dominated the far left in the 1920s through the 1950s, the far left became more diverse during the Cold War.
