[Eurocommunism](/wiki/Eurocommunism "Eurocommunism"), a gradualist and democratic approach to socialism, developed in the 1960s and was supported by the Communist Parties [of France](/wiki/French_Communist_Party "French Communist Party") and [of Italy](/wiki/Italian_Communist_Party "Italian Communist Party"). Other far-left ideologies formed their own [party families](/w/index.php?title=Party_families&action=edit&redlink=1 "Party families (page does not exist)").[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoughOlsenKoss20105-153)

Communist movements resurged in Central America during the 1940s and 1950s despite repression from authoritarian governments, and Marxism–Leninism lost influence among these groups.[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEÁlvarez2023b86–87-185) The [People's Vanguard Party](/wiki/People%27s_Vanguard_Party_(Costa_Rica) "People's Vanguard Party (Costa Rica)") in Costa Rica aligned with the winning coalition in the [1944 general election](/wiki/1944_Costa_Rican_general_election "1944 Costa Rican general election").[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEÁlvarez2023b86-145)

Following [Stalin's death](/wiki/Stalin%27s_death "Stalin's death"), the workers of several Eastern European countries staged revolutions against Communist rule, which were suppressed by the Soviet military.[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreslauer2021151-186) Many of these countries were led by Stalinist rulers, who were forced out and replaced by the subsequent Soviet government.[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreslauer2021153-187) Yugoslavia distanced itself as a neutral Communist nation, aligned with neither the East nor the West.[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreslauer2021154-188)

[Arab socialist](/wiki/Arab_socialist "Arab socialist") groups took power in the Middle East during the 1950s and 1960s, and they persecuted the preexisting Marxist groups to replace them as the region's predominant far-left movement.[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKraetzschmarResta2023415-143) The [Japanese Communist Party](/wiki/Japanese_Communist_Party "Japanese Communist Party"), supporting [scientific socialism](/wiki/Scientific_socialism "Scientific socialism"), was the far-left opposition to the dominant [Liberal Democratic Party](/wiki/Liberal_Democratic_Party_(Japan) "Liberal Democratic Party (Japan)") of Japan in the 1950s.[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGreene2010198-189) Indonesia's far left was destroyed in [a series of anti-communist mass killings](/wiki/Indonesian_mass_killings_of_1965%E2%80%9366 "Indonesian mass killings of 1965–66") in the mid-1960s, ending the [Communist Party of Indonesia](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Indonesia "Communist Party of Indonesia").[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUfen2023377-112)

The [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party") had been active since 1921, but it did not seize power in China until its victory in the [Chinese Civil War](/wiki/Chinese_Civil_War "Chinese Civil War") in 1949.[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreslauer2021117–118-190) As with the Soviet Union, the newly formed People's Republic of China carried out purges of political enemies, killing millions of landowners. The peasants were not targeted, however, instead using them as a base of political support.[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreslauer2021126-191) In the late 1950s and early 1960s, China under the rule of [Mao Zedong](/wiki/Mao_Zedong "Mao Zedong") distanced itself from the Soviet Union.[[191]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreslauer2021164–166-192) Maoism grew in popularity as an alternative to Soviet-style communism, but it did not result in any stable governments outside of China.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGray2023a100-32) At the same time, North Korea and [North Vietnam](/wiki/North_Vietnam "North Vietnam") were established as communist governments, triggering the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War") and the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War") against South Korea and [South Vietnam](/wiki/South_Vietnam "South Vietnam"), respectively.[[192]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreslauer2021135-193) By the late 1970s, Maoism in China was replaced by [the ideology](/wiki/Deng_Xiaoping_Theory "Deng Xiaoping Theory") of [Deng Xiaoping](/wiki/Deng_Xiaoping "Deng Xiaoping"), which restored the private sector and market pricing.[[193]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown2013381-194)

Many European Communist parties were fractured by different Marxist ideologies beginning in the 1950s, with the greatest challenges coming from Maoist and Trotskyist factions. Some European Communist parties saw Maoism as non-hierarchal and internationalist and adopted it for these reasons, but European Maoism only became a major force in the [Albanian Party of Labour](/wiki/Albanian_Party_of_Labour "Albanian Party of Labour").[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201136-175) Trotskyists gained influence among the European far left in the late 1960s and early 1970s, particularly in France and the United Kingdom, but it was plagued by inter-fighting, which limited its reach.[[194]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201137-195) The [Communist Party of Finland](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Finland "Communist Party of Finland") lost its influence in the 1960s as a schism emerged between pro-Soviet and pro-modernisation factions.[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201144-196)

Left-wing nationalist movements developed in colonial territories in the 1960s, leading to rapid decolonisation,[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201634-182) though traditional far-left ideas played a relatively small role in independence activities.[[196]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDaviesZdjelar2023a25-197)

Two major Latin American far-left ideologies emerged in the 1960s: [Guevarism](/wiki/Guevarism "Guevarism") and [Latin American liberation theology](/wiki/Latin_American_liberation_theology "Latin American liberation theology"). Guevarism emerged from the [Cuban Revolution](/wiki/Cuban_Revolution "Cuban Revolution"). The revolution's success dispelled the common belief among communists that socialist revolution could only occur after democratic and anti-imperialist reformist movements were successful.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEÁlvarez2023b88-34) Many guerrilla militant groups formed in Central America over the following decades, triggering the [Guatemalan Civil War](/wiki/Guatemalan_Civil_War "Guatemalan Civil War") and the [Salvadoran Civil War](/wiki/Salvadoran_Civil_War "Salvadoran Civil War").[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEÁlvarez2023b90–96-198) The [Sandinista National Liberation Front](/wiki/Sandinista_National_Liberation_Front "Sandinista National Liberation Front") led the [Nicaraguan Revolution](/wiki/Nicaraguan_Revolution "Nicaraguan Revolution") in 1979 and held power in Nicaragua until it was voted out in the [1990 general election](/wiki/1990_Nicaraguan_general_election "1990 Nicaraguan general election").[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEÁlvarez2023b93-199) [Latin American liberation theology](/wiki/Latin_American_liberation_theology "Latin American liberation theology") came amid the [Second Vatican Council](/wiki/Second_Vatican_Council "Second Vatican Council") and the [Second Episcopal Conference of Latin America](/wiki/Second_Episcopal_Conference_of_Latin_America "Second Episcopal Conference of Latin America"), popularising far-left ideas among some Catholic Churches in the region.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEÁlvarez2023b89-40)

In the mid-20th century, agricultural workers, the unemployed, and white-collar workers replaced industrial workers as the main far-left demographics in Western Europe. Highly educated people surpassed blue-collar workers as the primary far-left demographic by the end of the 1960s.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201647-122) The New Left developed in Western Europe as an alternative to orthodox Communism in the 1950s, taking positions on social issues and [identity politics](/wiki/Identity_politics "Identity politics").[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201135-67) It was unable to overcome traditional Communist parties except those of Scandinavia, where Communists were already sympathetic to ideas like Eurocommunism and humanism. [Communist Party of Denmark](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Denmark "Communist Party of Denmark") leader [Aksel Larsen](/wiki/Aksel_Larsen "Aksel Larsen") was expelled from his party for his opposition to the 1956 [Soviet invasion of Hungary](/wiki/Soviet_invasion_of_Hungary "Soviet invasion of Hungary"), so he formed the [Socialist People's Party](/wiki/Socialist_People%27s_Party_(Denmark) "Socialist People's Party (Denmark)") that became the new dominant far-left force in Danish politics.[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201136-175) The rise of the New Left was associated with the rise of [new social movements](/wiki/New_social_movements "New social movements") and the [counterculture of the 1960s](/wiki/Counterculture_of_the_1960s "Counterculture of the 1960s"), which also saw the revival of anarchism.[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKrüusselmannWeggemans2023a64–65-200) The Western far left as a whole resurged in the 1960s and 1970s as [American hegemony](/wiki/American_hegemony "American hegemony") and capitalist systems came under scrutiny. There were periods of civil unrest and youth revolts in several European nations.[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201634-182) The [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War") was a catalyst for New Left activity.[[200]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDaviesZdjelar2023a26-201) [Green politics](/wiki/Green_politics "Green politics") developed as an offshoot of the New Left, but it was [deradicalised](/wiki/Deradicalization "Deradicalization") by the end of the 20th century and became a centre-left movement.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201137–38-60)

New far-left socialist parties were formed across Western Europe, many Communist parties cut ties with the Soviet Union, and other Marxist movements such as [Maoism](/wiki/Maoism "Maoism"), [Trotskyism](/wiki/Trotskyism "Trotskyism"), and [workerism](/wiki/Workerism "Workerism") gained a presence in several countries' politics.[[201]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201635-202) Maoism significantly influenced far-left movements around the world in the 1970s and 1980s.[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESánchez-Cuenca2023a114-203) [Eurocommunism](/wiki/Eurocommunism "Eurocommunism") developed in response to the Soviet Union in the 1960s and 1970s to provide a democratic alternative for far-left ideas. It supports the expansion of European-style [welfare states](/wiki/Welfare_state "Welfare state") and mixed economies until they resemble communist society.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201650-30) The [People's Alliance](/wiki/People%27s_Alliance_(Iceland) "People's Alliance (Iceland)") of Iceland had remained electorally relevant by avoiding Soviet influence and adopted Eurocommunism to some success.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201142-36) The spread of Eurocommunism meant that Soviet-aligned communist parties declined in Western Europe.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201654-113) It proved unable to maintain its influence by the 1980s as its supporters were unable to reconcile vanguardism with political pluralism.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201142-36)

Smaller far-left groups revitalised interest in revolutionary communism in Western Europe.[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201649,_52-204) Maoist groups called the [K-Gruppen](/wiki/K-Gruppen "K-Gruppen") emerged from the [student movement in Germany](/wiki/Student_movement_in_Germany "Student movement in Germany").[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJesse2023a176-179) Maoist and Trotskyist groups briefly flourished in France in the 1960s amid the [French student movement](/wiki/Protests_of_1968 "Protests of 1968") and opposition to the [Algerian War](/wiki/Algerian_War "Algerian War").[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESommier2023a193-205) Italian Marxists like [Antonio Negri](/wiki/Antonio_Negri "Antonio Negri") introduced [autonomism](/wiki/Autonomism "Autonomism") later in the decade.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESommier2023a195-37) Support for the 1960s-era far left declined by the 1970s, giving violent revolutionaries more influence in the movement which further its decreased support.[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESánchez-Cuenca2023a110-206) [Earth First!](/wiki/Earth_First! "Earth First!") emerged in the 1980s in the United States, combining radical environmentalism and anarchism with other far-left social issues.[[206]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKrüusselmannWeggemans2023a65-207)[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMareš2023b282-74)

The [Communist and Allies Group](/wiki/Communist_and_Allies_Group "Communist and Allies Group") existed as a [transnational political party](/wiki/Transnational_political_party "Transnational political party") in the European Union during the 1970s and 1980s.[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch2011158-208) Far-left parties had representation in the Nordic countries during the Cold War.[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201642-151) In Sweden, a practice developed between the 1970s and the 1990s where social democrats would vote for the formerly Communist [Left Party](/wiki/Left_Party_(Sweden) "Left Party (Sweden)") so left-wing coalitions could be formed.[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201134-178) The [Portuguese Communist Party](/wiki/Portuguese_Communist_Party "Portuguese Communist Party") and the [Portuguese Democratic Movement](/wiki/Portuguese_Democratic_Movement "Portuguese Democratic Movement") played a major role in the [Carnation Revolution](/wiki/Carnation_Revolution "Carnation Revolution").[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201642-151) The French Communist Party was included in the French government of [François Mitterrand](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Mitterrand "François Mitterrand") in the early 1980s. This wave of European far-left support dissipated in the 1980s as workers lost influence in the economy, [neoliberalism](/wiki/Neoliberalism "Neoliberalism") became dominant, and the United States re-exerted influence over Europe.[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201634–36-209) As the economies of developed nations shifted, the far-left aligned with the workers of large corporations as opposed to small businesses and subcontractors.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201646-19)

Arabic far-left groups reemerged in the 1980s and 1990s, but they often aligned with the traditional authoritarian governments as a means to oppose [Islamism](/wiki/Islamism "Islamism"). This prevented them from creating a party structure and caused leftists to act through decentralised movements.[[209]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKraetzschmarResta2023416-210) Far-left [Arab socialists](/wiki/Arab_socialists "Arab socialists") were one of two groups alongside [Arab nationalists](/wiki/Arab_nationalists "Arab nationalists") that made up the [New Arab Left](/w/index.php?title=New_Arab_Left&action=edit&redlink=1 "New Arab Left (page does not exist)"), which began in Palestine and influenced other left-wing movements in the [Arab world](/wiki/Arab_world "Arab world").[[210]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJebari202123-211) [Hadash](/wiki/Hadash "Hadash") formed as a communist coalition in Israel with a focus on [the country's Arab population](/wiki/Arab_citizens_of_Israel "Arab citizens of Israel").[[211]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarreñada2021208-212) [Kurdish nationalism](/wiki/Kurdish_nationalism "Kurdish nationalism") emerged as the predominant far-left ideology in Turkey after [a period of political violence](/wiki/Political_violence_in_Turkey_(1976%E2%80%931980) "Political violence in Turkey (1976–1980)") and [the subsequent coup](/wiki/1980_Turkish_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1980 Turkish coup d'état") eradicated the previous leftist groups in 1980.[[212]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGumuscu2021194-213)

### Dissolution of the Soviet Union

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Far-left_politics&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Dissolution of the Soviet Union")]

Communist nations in Europe struggled economically in the 1980s, and many faced popular revolts.[[213]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201660–61-214) The Soviet Union moved away from ideas of international communism as such efforts came to be seen as too inconvenient.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch2011149-28) Soviet leader [Mikhail Gorbachev](/wiki/Mikhail_Gorbachev "Mikhail Gorbachev") effectively abandoned communism, leading the nation toward liberalism in its final years.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201143-88) The final Soviet-led international communist meeting was held in November 1987 to mark the seventieth anniversary of the October Revolution. It included not only communist parties, but other left-wing parties as the Soviet policy of socialism in one country disintegrated.[[214]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch2011149–150-215)

Communist and socialist parties severely declined in Western Europe after the [dissolution of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Soviet_Union "Dissolution of the Soviet Union").[[215]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVisserLubbersKraaykampJaspers2014541-216)[[216]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeithMarch2023173–174-217)[[217]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201661-218) Many communist parties were unable to survive once the Soviet Union no longer existed to finance them.[[218]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201146-219) The far-left was challenged by the [neoliberal consensus](/wiki/Neoliberal_consensus "Neoliberal consensus") after the dissolution of the Soviet Union. Intergovernmental institutions and increasing globalisation embedded neoliberal economics into the global economy, making it harder for the far-left to work against it.[[219]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201114–15-220) The [end of history](/wiki/End_of_history "End of history") theory was proposed by [Francis Fukuyama](/wiki/Francis_Fukuyama "Francis Fukuyama"), asserting that the neoliberal consensus effectively ended far-left politics[[220]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch20114–6-221) They remained largely irrelevant for several years until a period of regrowth toward the end of the 1990s.[[221]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201662–65-222) Many of the communist parties effectively disappeared from politics, while others rebranded or moderated.[[222]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201661,_72-223)[[216]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeithMarch2023173–174-217) The European transnational political party Communist and Allies Group split in 1989 as [Left Unity](/wiki/Left_Unity_(European_Parliament) "Left Unity (European Parliament)") and the [European United Left](/wiki/European_United_Left_(1989%E2%80%931993) "European United Left (1989–1993)"), and the latter merged with the [Nordic Green Left Alliance](/wiki/Nordic_Green_Left_Alliance "Nordic Green Left Alliance") to form [GUE/NGL](/wiki/GUE/NGL "GUE/NGL") in 1994.[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch2011158-208) The [Party of the European Left](/wiki/Party_of_the_European_Left "Party of the European Left") emerged in 2004.[[223]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch2011162-224)

In many Eastern European countries, communist parties were banned by the new governments.[[224]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201145-225) Most communist parties in Eastern Europe moved toward the centre-left.[[225]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201173-226) The [Socialist Party of Ukraine](/wiki/Socialist_Party_of_Ukraine "Socialist Party of Ukraine") was the only electorally relevant democratic socialist movement of Eastern Europe, but it also moved away from socialism over time.[[226]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch2011115-227) Those that remained communist held more influence than their counterparts in Western Europe.[[227]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201172-228) Moldova was an exception to the rejection of communism, where the communists won presidential elections throughout the 2000s.[[227]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201172-228)

As the social democratic vote was already contested by green parties and the New Left, formerly communist parties in Western Europe often shifted toward democratic socialism. Exceptions occurred in Italy and Poland, where the respective parties had already been moving toward social democracy.[[228]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201147-229) Surviving far-left parties shifted toward domestic politics and made attempts to distinguish themselves from Soviet-style communism.[[218]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201146-219) Parties like the [Communist Party of Greece](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Greece "Communist Party of Greece") maintained their adherence to traditional communism after the dissolution of the Soviet Union, while others like the [Communist Refoundation Party](/wiki/Communist_Refoundation_Party "Communist Refoundation Party") in Italy tried to introduce new communist ideas.[[229]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201151-230) The [Communist Party of the Russian Federation](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_the_Russian_Federation "Communist Party of the Russian Federation") and the [Party of Communists of the Republic of Moldova](/wiki/Party_of_Communists_of_the_Republic_of_Moldova "Party of Communists of the Republic of Moldova") remained relevant in the former Soviet Union, while the [Communist Party of Bohemia and Moravia](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Bohemia_and_Moravia "Communist Party of Bohemia and Moravia") emerged as the most prominent communist party in [East-Central Europe](/wiki/East-Central_Europe "East-Central Europe").[[227]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201172-228)

The far-left was able to rebuild limited support by the end of the decade.[[221]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201662–65-222)[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeithMarch2023176-80) Supporters of the far-left in Europe at this time were more likely to be professional workers, students, and the unemployed. The share of working class supporters declined as they sought other ideologies.[[230]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201673-231) It appealed to anti-neoliberalism and tried to rebuild ties with the working class.[[231]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201662-232) Most far-left parties in Europe prioritised a broader societal shift to the left instead of disputing individual policies.[[232]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201674-233) Far-left parties in Europe became more amenable to joining coalitions.[[233]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoughOlsenKoss20102-234) Many of them became more open to reformist politics as a temporary means to combat neoliberalism.[[234]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201616-235) Detailed platforms of societal reconstructions were avoided so as not to emulate Stalinism.[[235]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201672-236) The far-left primarily expressed itself through movements led by unions, pacifists, and [alter-globalisation](/wiki/Alter-globalisation "Alter-globalisation") advocates instead of traditional political parties.[[236]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201662–63-237) Over time, unions became less involved in these and social activism became more common.[[230]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201673-231) Despite his criticism of leftism, [Ted Kaczynski](/wiki/Ted_Kaczynski "Ted Kaczynski") became influential within green anarchism and anarcho-primitivism with his essay *[Industrial Society and Its Future](/wiki/Industrial_Society_and_Its_Future "Industrial Society and Its Future")* in the 1990s, which received attention because of Kaczynski's use of [letter bombs](/wiki/Letter_bomb "Letter bomb") to forward the cause.[[237]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZúquete2023c260-238)[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMareš2023b282-74)

Far-left parties reappeared in [post-Soviet states](/wiki/Post-Soviet_states "Post-Soviet states") in response to voter frustration with the new governments. Leftist parties in Russia and the [Balkans](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans") exchanged Marxism–Leninism for left-wing nationalism.[[238]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMazzoleniHeinisch202314–15-239) The Indonesian party system destabilised after the [fall of Suharto](/wiki/Fall_of_Suharto "Fall of Suharto") in 1998, and the traditional leftist electorate—trade unions and peasant associations–did not develop political representation.[[239]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUfen2023372-240)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Far-left_politics&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: 21st century")]

Of the five Communist states that survived into the 21st century, three of them — China, Vietnam, and Laos — had restored private ownership and reintegrated with global capitalist markets[[240]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBreslauer20211-241) although state and public control continued as well. For instance, [Peter Nolan](/wiki/Peter_Nolan "Peter Nolan") argues that land in China was decollectivized but not privatised, with control of land remaining in the hands of the community.[[241]](#cite_note-242)

At the start of the 21st century, the far-left was associated with the [global justice movement](/wiki/Global_justice_movement "Global justice movement") and supported populist leaders.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeithMarch2023174-15) This reached its height with the [1999 Seattle WTO protests](/wiki/1999_Seattle_WTO_protests "1999 Seattle WTO protests") and protests against the [27th G8 summit](/wiki/27th_G8_summit "27th G8 summit") in 2001,[[242]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch2011172-243) which symbolised a growing rejection of the neoliberal consensus.[[243]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch20116-244) The movement began holding the annual [World Social Forum](/wiki/World_Social_Forum "World Social Forum") in 2001. Its influence diminished following the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks"), but it survived as a leading force in [opposition to the Iraq War](/wiki/Opposition_to_the_Iraq_War "Opposition to the Iraq War").[[244]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch2011174-245)

Far-left violence decreased dramatically by the 21st century, with a limited presence remaining in developing nations and only a small number of isolated attacks in developed nations.[[245]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESánchez-Cuenca2023a116-246) The [Party of the European Left](/wiki/Party_of_the_European_Left "Party of the European Left") was established in 2004 as a pan-European political party for the far-left.[[223]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch2011162-224) The far-left parties during this time were rarely new creations, instead descending from earlier far-left parties of the 20th century.[[246]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch201124-247)[[247]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvan_der_Linden202225-248) Among the European [great powers](/wiki/Great_power "Great power"), Germany was the only one where the far-left made strong electoral performances in the 2000s, with the prominence of the [Party of Democratic Socialism](/wiki/Party_of_Democratic_Socialism_(Germany) "Party of Democratic Socialism (Germany)") and [WASG](/wiki/Labour_and_Social_Justice_%E2%80%93_The_Electoral_Alternative "Labour and Social Justice – The Electoral Alternative"), which merged to become [Die Linke](/wiki/Die_Linke "Die Linke") in 2007.[[248]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201681-249) While the Italian Communist Party was historically the most prominent communist party in Western Europe, the Italian far-left fractured and was dissolved into the centre-left in the 2000s.[[249]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti2016155–156-250) The French far-left did not face significant gains or losses as other European far-left groups did at the time.[[250]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti2016162-251) Left-wing populism experienced a surge at the start of the 21st century beginning with the rise of [Hugo Chávez](/wiki/Hugo_Ch%C3%A1vez "Hugo Chávez") and [Evo Morales](/wiki/Evo_Morales "Evo Morales") in Latin America.[[251]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarch2011118-252)

The environmentalist left was targeted by the United States government in [Operation Backfire](/wiki/Operation_Backfire_(FBI) "Operation Backfire (FBI)") during the [Green Scare](/wiki/Green_Scare "Green Scare") in the 2000s, in which environmentalist groups carried out attacks and acts of sabotage.[[237]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZúquete2023c260-238) The anonymous ultra-left works of the [Invisible Committee](/wiki/Invisible_Committee "Invisible Committee") in France were produced in 2008, advocating a unified resistance to capitalism during times of crisis.[[252]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBackes2023a13-253) [Individualists Tending to the Wild](/wiki/Individualists_Tending_to_the_Wild "Individualists Tending to the Wild") was formed in Mexico as a self-described [eco-terrorism](/wiki/Eco-terrorism "Eco-terrorism") group in the 2010s.[[253]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZúquete2023c267-254)

Leftist politics diminished in the Arab world by the 21st century as autocratic governments placed [token opposition](/wiki/Token_opposition "Token opposition") from leftist figures in the legislature.[[254]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJebari202125-255) Revolutionary left-wing politics were not prominent during the 2011 [Arab Spring](/wiki/Arab_Spring "Arab Spring"),[[255]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDel_Panta2021295-256) although socialist groups played a role e.g. in the [Egyptian revolution](/wiki/2011_Egyptian_revolution "2011 Egyptian revolution") and anarchist ideas were put into practice in the [local councils](/wiki/Local_Coordination_Committees_of_Syria "Local Coordination Committees of Syria") established as part of the [Syrian revolution](/wiki/Syrian_revolution "Syrian revolution"). In 2012, the autonomous region [Rojava](/wiki/Autonomous_Administration_of_North_and_East_Syria "Autonomous Administration of North and East Syria") in northwestern Syria established self-governance based on an anarchist direct democracy at the local level and a one-party state at the regional level.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The 2010s also saw a [global wave of protest movements](/wiki/Anti-austerity_movement "Anti-austerity movement") against [austerity](/wiki/Austerity "Austerity") and [finance capitalism](/wiki/Finance_capitalism "Finance capitalism"), including the [Occupy movement](/wiki/Occupy_movement "Occupy movement") and the [indignados](/wiki/Indignados "Indignados"), in which radical left ideas were prominent.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The anti-industrialist [Zadists](/wiki/Zadist "Zadist") became active in France in the 2010s as they occupied and squatted on the sites of planned development projects.[[256]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESommier2023a198–199-257) Far-left militant groups participated in [the war against](/wiki/War_against_the_Islamic_State "War against the Islamic State") the [Islamic State](/wiki/Islamic_State "Islamic State") in the 2010s.[[257]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKrüusselmannWeggemans2023a55-258) Anarchist and autonomist movements were active during the French [Yellow vests protests](/wiki/Yellow_vests_protests "Yellow vests protests") in 2018, where they competed with the far-right for control of the movement.[[258]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESommier2023a202–203-259)

#### Emergence and positioning of the populist left

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Far-left_politics&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Emergence and positioning of the populist left")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Demetris_Christofias_in_February_2011.jpg/250px-Demetris_Christofias_in_February_2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Demetris_Christofias_in_February_2011.jpg)

[Demetris Christofias](/wiki/Demetris_Christofias "Demetris Christofias"), the fourth general secretary of the Progressive Party of Working People, who served as the [President of Cyprus](/wiki/President_of_Cyprus "President of Cyprus") from 2008 to 2013

Far-left parties became more prominent in democratic systems following the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession").[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeithMarch2023173-1) There is disagreement as to whether this is associated with an overall increase in support.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeithMarch2023176-80) Some of these parties, such as [La France Insoumise](/wiki/La_France_Insoumise "La France Insoumise") in France, [Podemos](/wiki/Podemos_(Spanish_political_party) "Podemos (Spanish political party)") in Spain, and [Syriza](/wiki/Syriza "Syriza") in Greece, deliberately incorporated populism into their identity.[[259]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeithMarch2023181-260) At the same time, in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") and the [United States of America](/wiki/United_States "United States"), populist movements formed around [Jeremy Corbyn](/wiki/Jeremy_Corbyn "Jeremy Corbyn") and [Bernie Sanders](/wiki/Bernie_Sanders "Bernie Sanders").[[260]](#cite_note-:2-261) [Bernie Sanders](/wiki/Bernie_Sanders "Bernie Sanders") gained support from the far-left in the [2016 United States presidential election](/wiki/2016_United_States_presidential_election "2016 United States presidential election").[[261]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERooduijnBurgoonvan_Elsasvan_de_Werfhorst2017537-262)

When far-left parties took power, they were forced to work within national and international systems that prevented them from unilaterally changing the economic structure.[[262]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti2016203-263) They moved their focus away from the long-term goal of socialism so they could seek broader support from anti-neoliberal coalitions. They adopted support for a left-libertarian welfare state based on [Keynesianism](/wiki/Keynesianism "Keynesianism") and social justice as a temporary measure on the path toward socialist society.[[263]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti2016202-264)

The communist [Progressive Party of Working People](/wiki/Progressive_Party_of_Working_People "Progressive Party of Working People") controlled the government in Cyprus from 2008 to 2013.[[264]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti20162-265) Far-left parties in Greece, Portugal, and Spain made significant electoral gains in 2015, including Syriza taking control of the Greek government.[[264]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti20162-265) Gains beyond these countries were limited, as [right-wing populism](/wiki/Right-wing_populism "Right-wing populism") was instead boosted in other countries.[[265]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiocchetti201660-266)

Left-wing extremist activity is uncommon in 21st-century Europe.[[266]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDaviesZdjelar2023a37–38-267) It is even less common in Canada and the United States, where it aligns with movements like [Antifa](/wiki/Antifa_(United_States) "Antifa (United States)") and [Black Lives Matter](/wiki/Black_Lives_Matter "Black Lives Matter") as well as [radical environmentalist](/wiki/Radical_environmentalist "Radical environmentalist") and other social justice movements.[[267]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDaviesZdjelar2023a37,_41-268) The far-left became more prominent in the United States in opposition to [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") following the far-right [Unite the Right rally](/wiki/Unite_the_Right_rally "Unite the Right rally") in 2017.[[268]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZenn2023b53–54-269) While the American far-left had developed over the previous years as a movement for [economic justice](/wiki/Economic_justice "Economic justice"), it shifted toward [anti-racism](/wiki/Anti-racism "Anti-racism") as its primary cause, especially after the [murder of George Floyd](/wiki/Murder_of_George_Floyd "Murder of George Floyd") by police officers in 2020, which became a focal point of the anti-racism movement.[[269]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZenn2023b55–56-270)

The European far-left was split on the issue of [COVID-19 lockdowns](/wiki/COVID-19_lockdowns "COVID-19 lockdowns"), in which some found government measures to be oppressive but others counter-protested against far-right opposition to the lockdowns.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDaviesZdjelar2023a38-96)

## Far-left terrorism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Far-left_politics&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Far-left terrorism")]

Further information: [Left-wing terrorism](/wiki/Left-wing_terrorism "Left-wing terrorism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/RAF-Bombenanschlag_in_Ramstein%2C_1981.jpg/250px-RAF-Bombenanschlag_in_Ramstein%2C_1981.jpg)](/wiki/File:RAF-Bombenanschlag_in_Ramstein,_1981.jpg)

Aftermath of the [Red Army Faction](/wiki/Red_Army_Faction "Red Army Faction") (RAF) bombing attack of the [U.S. Air Forces Europe](/wiki/United_States_Air_Forces_in_Europe_%E2%80%93_Air_Forces_Africa "United States Air Forces in Europe – Air Forces Africa") headquarters at [Ramstein Air Base](/wiki/Ramstein_Air_Base "Ramstein Air Base"), [West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany") (1981)

Historically, violence has been widely accepted on the far left as a means to enact societal change.[[270]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESánchez-Cuenca2023a105-271) The [anti-authoritarian](/wiki/Anti-authoritarian "Anti-authoritarian") far left may consider violence justified in combatting repressive forces.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBackes2023a11-105) Revolutionary far-left groups might organise themselves into underground movements or carry out violent insurgencies. Insurgencies are only viable in developing nations where the state is not capable of defeating the group.[[271]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESánchez-Cuenca2023a106-272) According to political scientist [Ignacio Sánchez-Cuenca](/wiki/Ignacio_S%C3%A1nchez-Cuenca "Ignacio Sánchez-Cuenca"), as support for the far left decreases, violent groups shift their focus from attacks against the state to attacks for survival, such as freeing its members from prison.[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESánchez-Cuenca2023a110-206) However, many on the far left criticise the use of violence. For example, according to Sánchez-Cuenca, [Leon Trotsky](/wiki/Leon_Trotsky "Leon Trotsky") rejected the use of violence,[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] saying it does not benefit the working class like other measures such as striking.[[272]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESánchez-Cuenca2023a106–107-273) Far-left actors are rarely a major focus of [terrorism studies](/wiki/Terrorism_studies "Terrorism studies") in comparison to other motives like [jihadism](/wiki/Jihadism "Jihadism").[[273]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKrüusselmannWeggemans2023a56-274)

Many far-left student protest movements, typically associated with the New Left, formed violent revolutionary organisations between the 1960s and the 1980s. These included the [National Liberation Army](/wiki/National_Liberation_Army_(Colombia) "National Liberation Army (Colombia)") in Colombia, [Action Directe](/wiki/Action_Directe "Action Directe") in France, the [Red Army Faction](/wiki/Red_Army_Faction "Red Army Faction") in Germany, the [Red Brigades](/wiki/Red_Brigades "Red Brigades") in Italy, the [Japanese Red Army](/wiki/Japanese_Red_Army "Japanese Red Army"), [Shining Path](/wiki/Shining_Path "Shining Path") in Peru, the [Revolutionary Youth Federation of Turkey](/wiki/Revolutionary_Youth_Federation_of_Turkey "Revolutionary Youth Federation of Turkey"), and [Weather Underground](/wiki/Weather_Underground "Weather Underground") in the United States.[[274]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDaviesZdjelar2023a26,_34–35-275) Other violent far-left groups formed in response to democratisation of their respective countries, including [17N](/wiki/17N "17N") in Greece, [FP-25](/wiki/FP-25 "FP-25") in Portugal, and [GRAPO](/wiki/GRAPO "GRAPO") in Spain.[[275]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDaviesZdjelar2023a32–34-276) Left-wing militant groups were largely eradicated in the 1980s, and popular conceptions of terrorism began to focus on [jihadism](/wiki/Jihadism "Jihadism") instead of far-left politics.[[276]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDaviesZdjelar2023a36-277) Other far-left militant organisations of the time included the [CPI (Maoist)](/wiki/CPI_(Maoist) "CPI (Maoist)"), [Montoneros](/wiki/Montoneros "Montoneros"), [New People's Army](/wiki/New_People%27s_Army "New People's Army"), [Prima Linea](/wiki/Prima_Linea "Prima Linea"), and the [Tupamaros](/wiki/Tupamaros "Tupamaros").[[277]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERaufer1993-278)[[278]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChaliand2010227–257-279)[[279]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClark201830–42,_48–59-280) In 2021, the European Union considered the [Conspiracy of Fire Nuclei](/wiki/Conspiracy_of_Fire_Nuclei "Conspiracy of Fire Nuclei") in Greece and the [Informal Anarchist Federation](/wiki/Informal_Anarchist_Federation "Informal Anarchist Federation") in Italy to be the most serious far-left terrorism threats in Europe.[[280]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDaviesZdjelar2023a39-281)

According to Sánchez-Cuenca, 194 people were killed by anarchist attacks in the developed world between 1875 and 1925.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESánchez-Cuenca2023a107-138) Some attacks targeted political leaders, while others such as the [Liceu bombing](/wiki/Liceu_bombing "Liceu bombing") were directed at civilians.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESánchez-Cuenca2023a108-142) Most attacks carried out by radical environmentalists have targeted property, with attacks on individuals being rare.[[281]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMareš2023b281-282)

No far-left terrorist group or underground movement has ever been successful in fermenting a revolution; according to Sánchez-Cuenca all successful violent far-left revolutions[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] have been carried out by armed insurgent movements engaging in [guerrilla warfare](/wiki/Guerrilla_warfare "Guerrilla warfare").[[282]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESánchez-Cuenca2023a111-283) Insurgencies are able to control territory, protecting them from the state and giving them a population from which they can draw support.[[283]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESánchez-Cuenca2023a111–112-284) These insurgencies have been successful in nations such as [Cambodia](/wiki/Cambodia "Cambodia"), [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba"), [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal"), [Nicaragua](/wiki/Nicaragua "Nicaragua"), and [North Vietnam](/wiki/North_Vietnam "North Vietnam").[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESánchez-Cuenca2023a112-33) The anarchist concept of propaganda of the deed has historically been ineffective, according to Sánchez-Cuenca, failing to increase awareness of the anarchist cause or reducing support for it.[[284]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESánchez-Cuenca2023a109-285)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Far-left_politics&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-K-Gruppe_56-0)** The *[K-Gruppen](/wiki/K-Gruppen "K-Gruppen")* originally referred to the mainly [Maoist](/wiki/Maoist "Maoist")-oriented small parties and other associations that had emerged in the 1960s with the disintegration of the [Socialist German Student Union](/wiki/Sozialistischer_Deutscher_Studentenbund "Sozialistischer Deutscher Studentenbund") (SDS) and the associated decline of the [West German student movement](/wiki/West_German_student_movement "West German student movement"). The term "K group" has been used primarily by competing left groups as well as in the media. It served as a collective name for the numerous, often violently divided groups and alluded to their common self-image as communist cadre organizations. The German term Kader denotes the civil servants or party functionaries in autocratic state systems, especially in socialist states (today, among others, the People's Republic of China and Cuba). In the Soviet sphere of influence, cadres were a group of people in the party and ideology sector with political and technical knowledge and skills ("party cadres", "leadership cadres", "leadership cadres", "junior cadres", "cadre policy", "cadre management"). In particular, they included the functionaries of the parties and mass organizations (executives), and university and technical college graduates (experts), but not normal working people. The personnel department of a company was called "*Kaderabteilung*" in the GDR; the head of this department was called "*Kaderleiter*".

[ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-333-62265-0](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-333-62265-0 "Special:BookSources/0-333-62265-0"). "Farmland was 'de-collectivised' in the early 1980s. This was not followed by the establishment of private property rights in land. Because the CCP wished to prevent the emergence of a landlord class, it did not permit the purchase and sale of farmland. Still in 1994, the Party 'adhered to the collective ownership of farmland'. The village community remained the owner, controlling the terms on which land was contracted out and operated by peasant households. It endeavoured to ensure that farm households had equal access to farmland, while the village government obtained part of the Ricardian rents from the land to use for community purposes. The Chinese government, through the communist party remained substantially in control of the de-collectivisation of farmland. Farmland was not distributed via a free market auction, which would have helped to produce a locally unequal outcome. Rather the massively dominant form was distribution of land contracts on a locally equal per capita basis"
